scenario: Operate accumulates millions of completed instances. Slows queries. Wonders retention.
question: Process instance retention policy?

options.text: minHistoryCleanupInterval + maxHistoryCleanupInterval (ISO-8601 durations) -- cluster-wide cleanup cadence. - For ES/OS secondary storage: Operate's archiver + Elasticsearch ILM policies roll over and drop old indices on a schedule. enabled + cleanupMode -- Optimize-specific (separate cleanup pipeline for the BI tool's database). md:19-23 -- batch operations: Delete process / Delete decision, cascade-delete instances + Tasklist user tasks per :12-17. - For Operate performance: critical for high-volume -- combine cluster cleanup + Operate batch operations.

While technically possible, keeping millions of completed instances indefinitely causes Elasticsearch/OpenSearch index growth that degrades query performance in Operate, increases storage costs, and eventually makes the system unresponsive for operational queries. The scenario explicitly states queries are already slowing down, making a "keep forever" approach inadequate. Retention configuration is essential for production clusters with high process volume.

md, automated retention mechanisms also exist for production-scale deployments. For RDBMS secondary storage, cluster-level history cleanup intervals are configurable via Helm values. For Elasticsearch/OpenSearch secondary storage, Operate's archiver and ILM policies manage index lifecycle automatically. Relying solely on manual UI deletions does not scale for millions of instances in a busy production cluster.

Some automated retention mechanisms exist but require explicit configuration choices before they activate. The RDBMS history cleanup intervals must be set via Helm values using ISO-8601 duration properties. Elasticsearch ILM policies must be configured per index lifecycle requirements. Without explicit configuration, the default behavior is to retain all completed instance data indefinitely, which is what causes the query performance degradation described in this scenario. There is no zero-configuration automatic retention that works out-of-the-box without operator decisions.

a.text: Correct. Process instance retention: historyTimeToLive is Camunda 7 only -- NOT in Camunda 8: 0 hits for historyTimeToLive in components/modeler/bpmn/ or components/concepts/. Per-process retention NOT a BPMN-level attribute in C8 -- cluster-wide cleanup intervals are the canonical knob. Workaround for per-process retention semantics: tag processes via versionTag + use Operate Delete-process batch operations filtered by definition. Cluster-level retention: Configuration: Camunda Self-Managed: configure retention policies. Per cluster: not per process. Or: per process via attribute. For SaaS: Per plan: default retention. Customer may not control directly. What gets cleaned up: Completed process instances: History: tasks, variables, etc. Decision instances. Job logs. Active instances: Never cleaned up: still running. Failed instances: Depend on policy: similar treatment. For Operate performance: Indexes grow: More instances = more index data. Query latency increases. Cleanup reduces: Older data archived / deleted. Indexes smaller. Faster queries. For audit / compliance: May require longer retention. Conflicting with performance. Compromise: archive externally. Archive pattern: Before cleanup: export to data warehouse. Cleanup: from Camunda. Query archive for historical. For tiered storage: Hot: recent in Camunda. Warm: archived; queryable. Cold: long-term storage. Configuration approaches: Approach 1: Per-process TTL: Define on each BPMN. Different processes different TTLs. Approach 2: Cluster-wide default: Default for all processes. Override per-process if needed. Approach 3: External cleanup: Custom script. Delete via API. For "millions of instances": Strategies: Aggressive cleanup: 7-30 days TTL. Keep recent operational data. Archive then cleanup: ETL to data warehouse. Cleanup from Camunda. Sharded storage (advanced): Time-based indices. Drop old indices. For migration: Existing data: may need backfill. Apply retention retroactively. For testing retention: Verify cleanup runs: not just configured. Storage usage: trending down. For monitoring: Storage growth rate. Instance count over time. Cleanup activity. For data inventory: What's stored: per service. Retention per type. For incident handling: Failed instances: may want longer retention. For investigation. For audit: Cleanup events logged. What was deleted, when. For Camunda 8 specifics: Verify per version: Retention features: evolving. Default values. Override mechanisms. For Elasticsearch: Operate uses ES: per Set 16 Q52. Index lifecycle management (ILM): for time-based indices. Camunda-managed typically. For huge clusters: Partition cleanup: per partition. Parallel: faster. For "deleted but recoverable": Backups: still have data. Per Set 14 Q55: backup retention separate. For GDPR right to be forgotten: Personal data: remove on request. May trigger early cleanup. For long-running processes: Don't delete active: never. TTL only after completion. For variable size impact: Large variables = larger storage. Cleanup more impactful. For dev / staging vs prod: Dev: aggressive cleanup; clean state. Prod: balance with audit / debugging. For specific industries: Financial: longer retention. Healthcare: per regulations. Retail: shorter typical. For SaaS vs Self-Managed: SaaS: Camunda manages retention per plan. Self-Managed: full control + responsibility. For Operate index hygiene: ILM policies: roll over indices. Drop old indices: after retention. For audit trail outside Camunda: External audit log. Long-term immutable. Don't rely on Camunda for compliance long-term. Anti-patterns: No retention: storage explosion. Aggressive without archive: lose history. Manual cleanup: not scalable. Best practices: Define retention policy: based on need. Archive before cleanup: if needed. Automate: don't manual. Monitor + adjust. For the scenario (millions of instances): Aggressive cleanup: 7-30 days. Archive externally: for audit. Operate performance restored. For incremental implementation: 1. Document retention requirements. 2. Configure cluster / process TTL. 3. Implement archive pipeline. 4. Run cleanup. 5. Monitor.
